articles.title: Introducing Device Policy: Enforce Approved VS Code Extensions Across Your Fleet
articles.description: Device Policy lets security teams allow-list VS Code extensions and enforce it fleet-wide through Intune, Jamf, Kandji, or the DMG agent. No MDM required.

articles.title: Coordinated AsyncAPI Supply Chain Attack: Miasma RAT Delivered via Compromised CI/CD Pipelines in Two Repositories
articles.description: On July 14, 2026 at 07:10 UTC, three packages in the AsyncAPI generator monorepo (@asyncapi/generator@3.3.1, @asyncapi/generator-helpers@1.1.1, and @asyncapi/generator-components@0.7.1) were published to npm carrying an obfuscated dropper that fires the moment the library is loaded, not on install. The packages were published through the project's own legitimate GitHub Actions release workflow and carry valid npm OIDC provenance attestations, because the attacker didn't steal an npm token: they gained push access to the repository's next branch and let the project's real CI/CD pipeline do the publishing for them.

articles.title: Introducing Secret Exfiltration Protection for GitHub Actions
articles.description: StepSecurity now blocks and detects secret exfiltration in GitHub Actions, stopping attacks that plant malicious workflows to steal your repository secrets.

articles.title: jscrambler npm package publishes malicious preinstall binary
articles.description: On July 11, 2026, version 8.14.0 of jscrambler was published to npm carrying a malicious preinstall hook that drops and executes a platform-specific native binary on Linux, Windows, and macOS. jscrambler is the official CLI client for the Jscrambler Code Integrity API, a commercial JavaScript obfuscation and web-app protection service, with a clean version history dating back to 0.1.0. The compromised release was flagged by StepSecurity's AI Release Analyzer with a suspicion score of 0 (the maximum suspicion rating) on publish.

articles.title: Injective npm Supply Chain Attack: 18 Packages Backdoored to Steal Crypto Wallet Keys
articles.description: On July 8, 2026, attackers used access to a trusted developer's account to slip a backdoor into a widely used software development kit for the Injective blockchain. Disguised as harmless analytics, the code quietly captured wallet recovery phrases and private keys and sent them to an attacker-controlled server the moment a wallet was created or loaded. Automatic publishing pushed the tainted code out to 18 related packages within minutes, and it stayed live for less than an hour before being pulled and fixed. If your application installed any of the affected packages during that window, or picked up a cached copy since, treat any wallet secrets it touched as exposed.

articles.title: GitHub Secret Scanning Public Monitoring for Enterprises: Coverage and Gaps
articles.description: GitHub's new public monitoring finds your enterprise's leaked secrets anywhere on github.com. Here is what it covers, what it cannot see, and how to close the exfiltration gap
